objectives: Identify decision points where the flow of control branches on a condition.
Write exact Boolean conditions, including the correct comparison operator and logical operators.
Apply logical thinking to an unseen scenario, stating the outcome of both branches of each decision.

retrieval.a: A place where the flow of control branches depending on whether a condition is true or false.
The order in which a program's statements execute, shaped by its decision points.

retrieval.q: How many branches does a single IF/ELSE decision have?
retrieval.a: Two — one for the condition being true and one for it being false.

retrieval.q: Write the condition for 'lock the account after 3 failed attempts'.
Interleaving (2.1.3): how does a decision point relate to a sub-procedure's order?
retrieval.a: Decision points steer which steps run, while sequencing fixes order by data dependency; both control execution flow.

keywords.def: Identifying decision points where flow of control branches on a condition.
A place where flow branches on a condition, e.g. an IF, CASE or loop test.
An expression that evaluates to true or false, e.g. score >= 1000.
The order in which statements execute, altered by decision points.
One of the paths taken from a decision point; each decision has a true and a false branch.
A symbol such as <, <=, >, >=, = used to build a condition.
AND, OR or NOT, used to combine conditions, e.g. ticketValid AND feePaid.
A decision inside another, used when one test must take priority over another.

content.heading: Decision points and flow of control
points: A decision point branches the flow of control on a condition.
Flow of control is the order statements execute.
IF, CASE and loop tests are all decision points.

content.heading: Writing exact Boolean conditions
points: 'Over £50' is total > 50, so exactly £50 fails; use >= 50 to include it.
Choosing the right operator is where marks are won or lost.

content.heading: Both branches matter
points: Every decision has a true branch and a false branch.
Booking: seatsRequested <= seatsAvailable confirms; the false branch rejects the booking.
Always state what happens on both outcomes.

content.heading: Combining and nesting conditions
points: Use logical operators: barrier opens IF ticketValid AND feePaid.
Nest conditions when one test must override another.
Order nested tests so the priority condition is checked first.

content.heading: Exam technique
points: Give the exact Boolean, not vague wording like 'if it's fast'.
State the outcome of both branches.
Apply conditions to the scenario's actual variables.

worked_example.title: Choosing a streaming quality with logical thinking
steps: Identify the overriding decision: connection speed takes priority over subscription.
First condition: IF connectionSpeed < 5 (Mbps) THEN stream SD, regardless of subscription.
False branch of that test (connection adequate): move to the subscription decision.
Second condition: IF subscription = "premium" THEN stream 4K, ELSE stream HD.
State both branches of each: SD vs continue; 4K vs HD.
Note the conditions are nested and ordered so the speed test is checked first.

task: A car-park barrier should open only if the ticket is valid AND the fee has been paid. Write the exact Boolean condition using a logical operator, and state precisely what happens on both the true and false branches.
challenge: A game awards a bonus life at 1000 points and a 'game over' at 0 lives. Design the decision points for both, writing exact Boolean conditions and stating the outcome of every branch, and explain why the order they are tested in matters.

exit_ticket.q: A cinema discounts customers under 16. Write the exact condition.
A booking confirms when seatsRequested <= seatsAvailable. What happens on the FALSE branch?
exit_ticket.a: The booking is rejected and the user is told there are too few seats.

exit_ticket.q: A student writes IF total > 50 for free delivery. Does a £50 order qualify, and how would you fix it to include £50?
exit_ticket.a: No — 50 is not greater than 50; use IF total >= 50.
